one_line_takeaway: RECAP swaps expensive per-prompt gradient-based jailbreak optimization (GCG/PEZ/GBDA) for FAISS-based retrieval of pre-computed adversarial suffixes matched by intent category, cutting per-batch time from hours (GCG) or ~7 minutes (PEZ/GBDA) to about 4 minutes and enabling logits-free black-box use, at the cost of a lower attack success rate (33% vs. 59%/39%/35% on Vicuna 7B) that the author attributes to the retrieval database's current small size (226 entries).
key_finding: On Vicuna 7B (Table I), Average Success Rate was GCG (brute-force, 13 transferable tokens) 59%, PEZ 39%, GBDA 35%, RECAP 33% -- RECAP is close to but below all three gradient-based baselines. Average per-batch (20-25 prompt) completion time was PEZ 7.3 min, GBDA 7.1 min, GCG 8 hours (with GCG's brute-force transfer variant averaging 6.3 min), and RECAP 4 min -- the paper characterizes this as roughly 45% faster than the fastest gradient baseline (PEZ/GBDA) while GCG full training is ~2 orders of magnitude slower. On the hosted black-box Gemini API (harm filters off), RECAP achieved only 4-10% success, which the paper attributes to Gemini's separate prompt-guard filter and the model's far larger parameter count relative to the 7-8B models used for suffix generation; the paper notes the other baselines (GCG/PEZ/GBDA) cannot be run at all on this target because they need logits, so it presents RECAP's non-zero black-box result as the main practical advantage rather than raw ASR. The GCG-"jumpstart" experiment (seeding GCG optimization with RECAP tokens instead of random init) gave mixed results -- sometimes faster convergence at similar ASR, sometimes same training time with lower ASR -- and the author states this is inconclusive.
core_contribution: RECAP is a retrieval-based alternative to gradient-based adversarial suffix attacks (GCG, PEZ, GBDA) for jailbreaking LLMs. Instead of optimizing new adversarial tokens per prompt, the author pre-computes a database of (prompt, adversarial-suffix, per-algorithm success label) triples across 7 harm-intent categories, ranks GCG/PEZ/GBDA by their historical per-category success rate, and at inference time encodes an incoming prompt with a SentenceTransformer (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), retrieves the nearest neighbor(s) via a FAISS index, and appends the associated successful adversarial suffix from the highest-ranked algorithm for that prompt's intent category. This avoids per-prompt gradient computation/training and does not require access to target-model logits, so it can in principle be applied to black-box hosted models.
threat_model: Attacker (or red-teamer) has only text-level (black-box) access to a target LLM's chat interface -- no logits, gradients, or internal activations required at inference time. The attacker's goal is to elicit harmful/policy-violating outputs by appending an adversarial suffix retrieved from a pre-built database to a user query, using a similarity-search match on the query's embedding rather than per-query optimization. The paper frames this explicitly as a red-teaming/penetration-testing tool for evaluating LLM guardrails, while also acknowledging dual-use risk (the same suffixes could be used to actually jailbreak a deployed model).
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the author: (1) the retrieval database is small (226 filtered rows from 780 candidates), which the author states is the primary reason RECAP's success rate trails the trained baselines, with improvement expected only as the database grows; (2) RECAP's ASR (33%) is strictly lower than GCG (59%), PEZ (39%), and GBDA (35%) on the primary Vicuna 7B benchmark -- it is not actually 'comparable or superior' in this table despite the abstract's phrasing; (3) black-box transfer to Gemini yields only 4-10% success, far below the open-model results; (4) the GCG-jumpstart extension produced inconsistent results the author could not explain; (5) intent labeling was done automatically by a single prompted Vicuna-7B classifier with no reported human validation or inter-rater agreement; (6) evaluation relies entirely on the HarmBench classifier as ground truth for 'success,' with no manual/human verification reported; (7) the study is single-author, uses a fixed set of 13 pre-selected transferable GCG tokens rather than fresh GCG training as the strong baseline for speed comparisons (biasing GCG's reported time down for the transfer variant), and target models are limited to Llama 3 8B / Vicuna 7B / Phi plus one black-box test on Gemini -- no statistical significance testing or confidence intervals are reported for any ASR number.

datasets_used: Public dataset of harmful/policy-violating prompts used to build the 780-row (filtered to 226-row) RECAP retrieval database, auto-labeled into 7 intent categories by a prompted Vicuna-7B classifier
LibrAI Do-Not-Answer dataset (Hugging Face) -- source of test prompts
IndicAlign dataset (Hugging Face) -- source of test prompts
145 test prompts total, randomly shuffled from the combination of Do-Not-Answer and IndicAlign
quantitative_results: Table I ASR on Vicuna 7B: GCG (brute-force) 59%, PEZ 39%, GBDA 35%, RECAP 33%
Table I average time per batch (20-25 prompts) on Vicuna 7B: GCG brute-force transfer 6.3 min (full GCG training ~8 hours), PEZ 7.3 min, GBDA 7.1 min, RECAP 4 min
Preliminary run (145 prompts, per-batch of 20-25) average success rates reported in the intro: GBDA 0.39, GCG 0.59, RECAP 0.33; per-batch generation time GBDA ~7 min, GCG ~3 hours, RECAP ~4 min
RECAP success rate on black-box Gemini API (harm filters disabled): 4% to 10%
Database filtering: 780 rows reduced to 226 rows after keeping only prompts with at least one successful adversarial variant across GCG/PEZ/GBDA
GCG used a fixed list of 13 transferable adversarial tokens rather than per-prompt training
baselines_compared: GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) -- Zou et al. 2023, used via 13 pre-selected transferable tokens (brute-force transfer) rather than fresh per-prompt training
PEZ (referred to in the References list as 'PromptRobust' by Zhu et al., but used in-text as the PEZ prompt-embedding-quantization attack)
GBDA (Gradient-Based Distributional/Dynamic Attack) -- Guo et al. 2021
Jailbreak and ZeroShot methods are named as comparison points in the Experiment Results intro paragraph but no numeric results for them are given in Table I or elsewhere in the extracted text
